revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kotohiki Hachiman-gū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | width = | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachiman-gū | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachiman-gū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the City.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012}} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] {{Japan-struct-stub}} [[ja:琴弾八幡宮]]

The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the City.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012}} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord missing|Japan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] {{Japan-struct-stub}} [[ja:琴弾八幡宮]]

revision.comment: Hijiri88 moved page [[Kotohiki Hachiman-gū]] to [[Kotohiki Hachimangū]]: Per change in MOS. (I didn't unilaterally change MOS just now. It was discussed and the change in wording was implemented years ago, but never imposed on the articles.)

[[Tamayori-bime]] | founded = 703 | closed = | founder = | priest = | location_map = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | address = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the City.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 |df= }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kotohiki Hachimangu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] {{Japan-struct-stub}}

The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the City.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kotohiki Hachimangu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:703]] [[Category:8th-century religious buildings and structures]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kotohiki Hachimangu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:703]] [[Category:8th-century religious buildings and structures]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Dd]][[en:Kotohiki Hachimangū]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Hachiman Faith}}

The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kotohiki Hachimangu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:703]] [[Category:8th-century religious buildings and structures]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Kotohiki Hachimangū]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Hachiman Faith}}

The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kotohiki Hachimangu}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:703]] [[Category:8th-century religious buildings and structures]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:New Shikoku Mandala Sacred Sites]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipediawikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kotohiki Hachimangū]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Hachiman Faith}}

[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia [[Empress Jingū]]

Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ 琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Kotohiki Hachimangū]] 2]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Kotohiki Hachimangū]] 2]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Kotohiki Hachimangū]] 2]][[category:kk]][[category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} 琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from 琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from 琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buqqhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buqqhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buqqhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buqqhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buqqhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buqqhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buqqhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buqqhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kotohiki Hachimangū
琴弾八幡宮 | image = Kotohiki-hachiman entrance.jpeg | caption = [[Torii]] of Kotohiki Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.131613|133.647799|format=dms|display=title,inline|type:railwaystation_region:JP}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman]] shrine | deity = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia [[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Matsuri|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]] 2]] 2]] 2]][[Category|Kotohiki-hachiman-gū}} * [[Hachiman shrine]] * [[Shikoku 88 temple pilgrimage]] * [[Shinbutsu bunri]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] 2]] [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Japanese festivals|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]] 2]] 2]] [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. Located within [[Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[:en:Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]] | founded_by = | established = 703 | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Yahata-chō 1-chōme, [[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]] [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kotohiki Hachimangū'''|琴弾八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Located within [[:en:Kotohiki Park|Kotohiki Park]], itself part of the [[Setonaikai National Park]], there is an [[:en:Etiology|aetiological legend]] that sees the god [[Hachiman]] appearing to the eighth-century monk Nisshō Shōnin while he was playing a [[:en:Koto (instrument)|koto]] on board ship. The legend is depicted in the ''[[Sanuki Province|Sanuki-no-kuni]] Shippōzan Hachiman Kotobikigū engi'', records that are an [[Tangible Cultural Properties of Japan#Designated Tangible Cultural Properties|Important Cultural Property of the city.]]{{cite book |title=The 88 Temples of Shikoku Island, Japan |pages=123f |author=Miyata, Taisen |publisher=Koyasan Buddhist Temple, Los Angeles |year=2006}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |title=List of Cultural Properties in Kan'onji City |publisher=[[:en:Kan'onji, Kagawa|Kan'onji City]] |accessdate=8 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150220203305/http://www.city.kanonji.kagawa.jp/info/k12/070208-2.html |archivedate=20 February 2015 }} As a result of the [[Shinbutsu bunri|enforced separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] during the [[Meiji period]], the enshrined image of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] was transferred to nearby [[Kannon-ji]].{{cite book |title=Shikoku henro |author=Hirahata Yoshio |publisher=Manganji Kyōkabu |page=218 |year=2010 |language=Japanese }} There is a lively [[Japanese festivals|annual festival]].{{cite web |url=http://www.chousa.net/ |title=Kotohiki Hachimangū Daisai / Kannon-ji Chōsa Matsuri |publisher=Chōsa Matsuri |accessdate=8 February 2012}} ==See also== {{Commons Category:11]] 2]] 2]]
